examples.title: Bootstrap Loading Algorithm
examples.description: Simplified pseudocode showing bootstrap process steps
examples.code:
ALGORITHM Bootstrap
BEGIN
  POWER_ON:
    Initialize CPU registers to known state
    SET program_counter to ROM_BIOS_ADDRESS
    
  POST:
    EXECUTE Power-On Self-Test
    CHECK memory integrity
    DETECT hardware devices
    
  LOAD_BOOTSTRAP:
    READ bootstrap_loader from fixed ROM address
    TRANSFER control to bootstrap_loader
    
  OS_LOAD:
    LOCATE boot_device (hard disk, SSD)
    READ first sector (MBR/VBR)
    LOAD OS kernel into memory address 0x100000
    
  KERNEL_INIT:
    SETUP page tables and memory management
    INITIALIZE interrupt descriptor table
    DETECT and initialize all hardware devices
    LOAD device drivers
    START system daemon processes
    
  USER_ENVIRONMENT:
    MOUNT file systems
    START login services (getty/display manager)
    WAIT for user login
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates the bootstrap sequence from power-on to OS ready state, showing initialization of hardware, loading kernel, and preparing user environment.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of hardware components
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) constant memory for bootstrap itself

examples.title: System Call Simulation - Process Creation
examples.description: Demonstrates how fork() system call works to create new processes
examples.code:
import os
import sys

# Simulating fork() system call behavior
def simulate_fork():
    """
    Demonstrates process creation using fork() system call.
    In actual OS, fork() creates an exact copy of parent process.
    """
    print("Parent Process: About to fork a child process")
    
    # This represents the fork() system call
    # Returns twice: once in parent (child PID), once in child (0)
    child_pid = os.fork()
    
    if child_pid == 0:
        # Child process execution
        print(f"Child Process: I am the child (PID: {os.getpid()})")
        print("Child Process: Executing new program...")
        # In real OS, this would be exec() system call
        sys.exit(0)
    elif child_pid > 0:
        # Parent process execution
        print(f"Parent Process: Child created with PID: {child_pid}")
        # Wait for child to complete
        os.waitpid(child_pid, 0)
        print("Parent Process: Child has terminated")
    else:
        # Fork failed
        print("Error: Fork failed")

# Simulating system call interface
def system_call_example():
    """
    Shows how system calls work at interface level.
    In real OS, these would invoke kernel functions.
    """
    # System call: getpid - get process ID
    pid = os.getpid()
    print(f"System Call getpid() returned: {pid}")
    
    # System call: getuid - get user ID
    uid = os.getuid()
    print(f"System Call getuid() returned: {uid}")
    
    # System call: write - output to file descriptor
    # fd=1 is standard output
    os.write(1, b"System Call write() to stdout\n")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    print("=== System Call Simulation ===")
    system_call_example()
    print("\n=== Process Creation ===")
    simulate_fork()
examples.explanation: This Python code simulates operating system process management. The fork() system call creates a new process by duplicating the parent process. The os.getpid(), os.getuid(), and os.write() demonstrate various system calls that interface with the OS kernel.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for getpid/getuid, O(n) for fork where n is process creation overhead
examples.spaceComplexity: O(k) where k is process control block size for new process

examples.title: Interrupt Handler Implementation
examples.description: Shows how interrupt handlers are implemented in C
examples.explanation: This C code demonstrates how interrupt handlers work in operating systems. The timer_isr function shows an Interrupt Service Routine (ISR) that saves CPU state, handles the interrupt, sends EOI to PIC, and restores state. The sys_read function demonstrates a system call handler with privilege checking.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) fixed stack space for interrupt handling

examples.title: Memory Allocation System Call
examples.description: Pseudocode for memory allocation system call (brk/sbrk)
examples.code:
ALGORITHM Memory_Allocation_Request
INPUT: requested_size (bytes)
OUTPUT: memory_address or error code

BEGIN
  // Step 1: Validate request
  IF requested_size < 0 OR requested_size > MAX_MEMORY THEN
    RETURN ERROR_INVALID_SIZE
  END IF
  
  // Step 2: Check current process privileges (dual-mode)
  IF current_mode = USER_MODE THEN
    // User can only request limited memory
    IF requested_size > USER_MAX_ALLOCATION THEN
      RETURN ERROR_PERMISSION_DENIED
    END IF
  END IF
  
  // Step 3: Check available memory
  available = GET_AVAILABLE_PHYSICAL_MEMORY()
  IF requested_size > available THEN
    // Try to free some memory or swap
    ATTEMPT_MEMORY_SWAP(requested_size)
    IF NOT SUCCESSFUL THEN
      RETURN ERROR_OUT_OF_MEMORY
    END IF
  END IF
  
  // Step 4: Find suitable memory block
  // Using first-fit algorithm
  FOR EACH block IN memory_map DO
    IF block.status = FREE AND block.size >= requested_size THEN
      // Found suitable block
      IF block.size > requested_size + MIN_BLOCK_SIZE THEN
        // Split the block
        new_block = block + requested_size
        new_block.size = block.size - requested_size
        new_block.status = FREE
        INSERT_INTO_LIST(memory_map, new_block)
      END IF
      
      block.size = requested_size
      block.status = ALLOCATED
      block.owner_pid = current_process.pid
      
      // Update process memory info
      current_process.memory_used = current_process.memory_used + requested_size
      
      RETURN block.address
    END IF
  END FOR
  
  // Step 5: No suitable block found - expand heap
  new_address = EXTEND_HEAP(requested_size)
  IF new_address = NULL THEN
    RETURN ERROR_OUT_OF_MEMORY
  END IF
  
  RETURN new_address
END

ALGORITHM Memory_Free
INPUT: memory_address
OUTPUT: success or error code

BEGIN
  // Validate address belongs to current process
  IF NOT VALIDATE_OWNERSHIP(current_process, memory_address) THEN
    RETURN ERROR_INVALID_ADDRESS
  END IF
  
  // Find the allocated block
  block = FIND_BLOCK(memory_address)
  
  // Mark block as free
  block.status = FREE
  
  // Update process memory info
  current_process.memory_used = current_process.memory_used - block.size
  
  // Coalesce adjacent free blocks (optional optimization)
  COALESCE_FREE_BLOCKS(memory_map)
  
  RETURN SUCCESS
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates how memory allocation system calls work in operating systems. It shows validation, privilege checking, memory availability checking, allocation algorithm, and the corresponding free operation with block coalescing.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for scanning memory map, O(1) for simple allocation
examples.spaceComplexity: O(k) where k is number of memory blocks in the map
